q: Best advice you've ever received?
a: Ask the difficult questions, they make a relationship stronger; it could break it or make it, but you gain understanding.

q: Worst advice you've ever received?
a: Being told to wear a certain outfit or drop a certain song, you never really know how it'll land.

q: Finish the sentence: 'One thing I'll never apologize for is...'
a: My mouth, though sometimes I admit I am a little too hard and do apologize.

q: Something you used to value that you don't anymore?
a: The streets, it gave me my mentality, but I'd never advise it to kids or people I love.

q: If you could create one law everyone had to follow, what would it be?
a: Harsh, mandatory punishment for anyone who hurts children, kids are precious.

q: Best advice you've ever received?
a: Do it scared.

q: Worst advice you've ever received?
a: Doesn't have one, he blocks bad advice out.

q: First thing you do in the morning and last thing at night?
a: Morning: get his cats off the bed. Night: move his feet so the cats won't scratch them.

q: Something you used to value that you don't anymore?
a: Approval from everyone around him.

q: What advice would you give your younger self? (asked in place of the law question)
a: Make sure you love yourself enough, more than anybody else in the world ever could.

q: Best advice you've ever received?
a: Stay humble, be grateful, and work hard (from his father).

q: Worst advice you've ever received?
a: 'Don't make this mistake', you have to make mistakes to learn and grow from them.

q: How do you prepare on race day and end the day after a race?
a: Cold baths, he starts and ends the day that way.

q: Something you've learned about yourself through Formula 1?
a: That he's much stronger than he initially thought.

q: If you could create one law everyone had to follow, what would it be?
a: Be nice and respectful to everybody.

q: Best advice you've ever received?
a: Focus on yourself and don't listen to people, because most people want you to fail (from her father).

q: Worst advice you've ever received?
a: Can't remember any, bad things never stay in her head.

q: Your three most life-changing matches?
a: Her US Open semifinal comeback vs. Madison Keys, her first Grand Slam win, and a Doha comeback, the matches where she was down and fought back.

q: Looking back on your career one day, what would you like to feel about it?
a: That she did everything she could, inspired young kids, and is remembered in the sport's history.

q: If you could create one law everyone had to follow, what would it be?
a: Decide everything through conversation, sit down, talk, and don't leave the room until problems are solved.

q: Best advice you've ever received?
a: Don't be afraid to fail, nobody remembers the misses if you keep going.

q: Worst advice you've ever received?
a: Being told to fit in and copy other producers' styles when he was young.

q: Something you used to value that you don't anymore?
a: Success, chasing the No. 1 song; now he creates for himself, not as a competition.

q: A lesson from your parents' relationship you carry forward?
a: Listen to each other, and accept that it's okay to be wrong instead of defending it.

q: If you could create one law everyone had to follow, what would it be?
a: That people talk things out, love one another, and live together without fighting or violence.

q: Best advice you've ever received?
a: If you're unsure about something, take a moment, don't answer right away just to be quick.

q: Worst advice you've ever received?
a: 'Buy it now, you'll make it back', spend now, you'll earn it back.

q: What advice would you give your younger self?
a: Savor those years, enjoy the purity and innocence of being a kid.

q: Best part of being Michael B. Jordan?
a: Being 'Uncle Mike' to his niece and nephew.

q: Worst part of being Michael B. Jordan?
a: The loneliness and isolation that come with the responsibility.

q: Best advice you've ever received?
a: The most important things you learn, you won't learn in the classroom, go have experiences (from his father).

q: Worst advice you've ever received?
a: 'Follow your passion', better to think long-term about the skills and experiences you need.

q: An experience you never thought you'd try but are glad you did?
a: Psychedelics, some of his most intense spiritual experiences.

q: An experience you're excited to try this year?
a: Going to Wimbledon with his son.

q: If you could create one law everyone had to follow, what would it be?
a: Effective mandatory age verification on the internet, so kids can't freely access porn and similar sites.

q: Best wealth advice you've ever received?
a: Find your talent.

q: Worst wealth advice you've ever received?
a: Follow your passion.

q: How would you define your current purpose?
a: To raise loving, patriotic men.

q: Is it possible to make peace with money?
a: Yes, money is the means, not the end; it should remove the stress that blocks deep, meaningful relationships.

q: Best advice you've ever received?
a: If you want to shrink your problems, grow your purpose.

q: Worst advice you've ever received?
a: You're perfect the way you are.

q: Something you used to value that you don't anymore?
a: Wealth.

q: How would you define your current purpose?
a: God-given.

q: If you could create one law everyone had to follow, what would it be?
a: Pause before you speak.

q: Best advice you've ever received?
a: Take action as soon as you wake up, instead of scrolling your phone.

q: Worst advice you've ever received?
a: That constantly drinking alcohol is normal and isn't harming you.

q: Something you're trying to value less?
a: Social media following, chasing the dopamine of more followers instead of real service.

q: Something you're trying to value more?
a: Phone-free, intimate time with his partner.

q: If you could create one law everyone had to follow, what would it be?
a: Everyone spends 60 minutes alone in nature every day, without their phone.

q: Best advice you've ever received?
a: Find what you really enjoy doing, if you love it, that's hard to beat.

q: Worst advice you've ever received?
a: 'Don't take risks', failure is a better teacher; we've over-prioritized safety.

q: The most important problem the smartest people should be solving?
a: Shaping AI (alongside avoiding nuclear war and bioterrorism, climate change, and helping the poorest).

q: Your favorite mental health habit?
a: Time reading and thinking alone, his 'think weeks' and long solo walks.

q: The book you've gifted most?
a: 'The Better Angels of Our Nature' by Steven Pinker.

q: Best advice you've ever received?
a: Be yourself.

q: Worst advice you've ever received?
a: Act like this other person.

q: A lesson you learned the hard way?
a: To be a better parent, she improved when she stopped being dictatorial and really listened to her kids.

q: A message you want to leave behind?
a: There's so much potential in everybody; we need to break down the barriers and biases that keep it locked.

q: If you could create one law everyone had to follow, what would it be?
a: Anywhere you see a man in a position of power, he'd be accompanied by a woman or a person of color.

q: Best advice you've ever received?
a: Learn to say 'that's none of my concern', there's power in not needing to engage with everything.

q: Worst advice you've ever received?
a: 'Just do it' (the Nike slogan), better to first ask whether you really want to do it.

q: How would you define your current purpose?
a: To let it be and accept whatever it is, and realize how amazing it is just to be alive.

q: What does it feel like when Tom Cruise tells you your life is more valuable than his?
a: It feels like a lie, but Tom could say anything and he'd believe him.

q: If you could create one law everyone had to follow, what would it be?
a: For every minute you spend on social media, you're locked out of the app for that many minutes.

q: Best advice you've ever received?
a: Have respect from the doorman to the director, respect everyone (from his mother).

q: Worst advice you've ever received?
a: He'd blocked it out and couldn't recall any.

q: First thought in the morning and last at night?
a: A family gratitude in their family bed, 'Thank you, God, for today, I'm grateful', and the same at night.

q: Something you used to value that you don't anymore?
a: Other people's opinions of him.

q: If you could create one law everyone had to follow, what would it be?
a: Respect, a law of respecting all life.

q: Best advice you've ever received?
a: Don't let anybody in your house who has nothing to lose.

q: Worst advice you've ever received?
a: A man telling her 'I don't have to wear a condom, you know I love you', that's not love.

q: Your most repeated thought on a daily basis?
a: 'Clean your room.'

q: Something you used to value that you don't anymore?
a: Saving checks, she used to keep them in a photo album.

q: If you could create one law everyone had to follow, what would it be?
a: You have to hug someone every day.

q: Best life advice you've ever received?
a: Be useful (an Arnold Schwarzenegger line).

q: Worst advice you've ever received?
a: To scrap the Reddit alien and rename Reddit 'Octopop', he ignored it (from Paul Graham).

q: Advice for parents juggling business and family?
a: Build routines and traditions, discipline is freedom.

q: How would you define your current purpose?
a: Enabling a whole new generation of entrepreneurs to do bigger and better things than he did.

q: If you could create one law everyone had to follow, what would it be?
a: Everyone should get to experience the joy of making someone they truly love happy.

q: Best life advice you've ever received?
a: Tell the truth, or at least don't lie.

q: Worst advice you've ever received?
a: It's all about you.

q: How would you define your current purpose?
a: To keep exploring the limits of possibility.

q: How do we scale trust?
a: By practicing honesty in your own life and rewarding it in the people you interact with.

q: If you could create one law everyone had to follow, what would it be?
a: Don't follow stupid rules, but be willing to bear the consequences of objecting.

q: Best advice you've ever received?
a: Spend so much time on self-improvement that you have no time to criticize others.

q: Worst advice you've ever received?
a: No one taught him how to spend or invest the money, he wishes he'd gotten advice on managing it.

q: The unexpected gift of parenting?
a: Life is no longer about him, coming home to just be 'daddy' is the most grounding thing.

q: What surprised you most about the music industry in 20 years?
a: How completely technology changed it, about 120,000 new songs are released every day.

q: If you could create one law everyone had to follow, what would it be?
a: Do one random act of kindness every day.

q: Best health or longevity advice you've ever received?
a: Build habits.

q: Worst health or longevity advice you've ever received?
a: Cheat days, they teach bad habits and set unrealistic expectations.

q: What do you do for fun?
a: Outdoor adventure, dog-sledding in the Arctic, racing in the Moroccan desert, visiting a volcano in Iceland.

q: What data tools do you recommend to track your health?
a: His Blueprint protocol, blood work, speed of aging, 11 organ ages, full-body scan and microplastics testing.

q: If you could create one law everyone had to follow, what would it be?
a: To not follow laws, reframe society from 'don't do' rules to 'how to' guidance.

q: Best advice you've ever received?
a: Nothing specific, more an energy: anything someone says that creates more space.

q: Worst advice you've ever received?
a: To 'suck it up.'

q: How would you define your current purpose?
a: Being committed to being the most honest version of himself in every moment.

q: Your message to fans with this album?
a: Bring your tissues, he loves them, is grateful for their patience, and always wants to be honest with them.

q: If you could create one law everyone had to follow, what would it be?
a: Say what you really feel, but in a way that doesn't hurt people, speak your truth.

q: Best entrepreneurship advice you've ever received?
a: Don't be afraid to fail, if you're afraid, you'll never take the shot.

q: Worst entrepreneurship advice you've ever received?
a: Do it for the money.

q: Something you're currently trying to learn?
a: Complete consumer focus, doing what's best for the customer in everything.

q: How would you define your current purpose with your impact work?
a: Real change, measure the results and run it like a business, not a charity.

q: If you could create one law everyone had to follow, what would it be?
a: No one can be U.S. President after age 65, he wants a president with an animal-level work ethic.

q: Best health advice you've ever received?
a: Eat like you love yourself.

q: Worst health advice you've ever received?
a: 'Carbs are your enemy', your body needs carbs that come from the Earth.

q: Something you used to believe about health but don't anymore?
a: That someone else has the answers to your body, real healing came from embodiment and self-trust.

q: First thing you do in the morning and last at night?
a: Meditate in gratitude for her heart.

q: If you could create one law everyone had to follow, what would it be?
a: Lead with empathy, you don't know the other person's story.

q: Best health or fitness advice you've ever received?
a: Stay true to yourself and your uniqueness, and for fitness, just start.

q: Worst advice you've ever received?
a: 'Stop lifting weights, it'll make you bulky and masculine.'

q: Your hope for people this year?
a: To do everything from a place of love, for themselves and everyone around them.

q: First thing you do in the morning and last at night?
a: First she meditates; last, she says her gratitudes.

q: If you could create one law everyone had to follow, what would it be?
a: Allow, stop needing to control everything and let the good and love in.

q: Best advice you've ever received about recharging?
a: Be present, you drain your battery when your mind has too many 'tabs' open.

q: Worst advice you've ever received about recharging?
a: His mom's 'power through', it's really about powering up, not powering through.

q: Your favorite ways to power up?
a: No phone first thing; start coffee, open a window and take three breaths; walking meetings; daily exercise.

q: Your favorite ways to power down?
a: Turn everything off, phone out of the room, laptop disconnected from messaging apps.

q: If you could create one law everyone had to follow, what would it be?
a: Teach kids about their own mental health in school, the way we teach gym.

q: Best advice you've ever received?
a: Everything is your fault, you're the CEO of your own life.

q: Worst advice you've ever received?
a: Fit in with everybody else.

q: Your most repeated thought recently?
a: 'I'm proud of you.'

q: The number-one daily habit that changes your life?
a: Meditation and breath work, finding space for himself.

q: If you could create one law everyone had to follow, what would it be?
a: Take five minutes every day to practice self-awareness.

q: Tom Hanks gifted you a typewriter and you became pen pals writing in character. How did that serve as a character development exercise?
a: Writing letters as Elvis, especially on a typewriter you cannot easily edit, forced me to think like him and expanded the memories I carried into the role.

q: When you are watching films, can you tell how an actor is trained, or is the whole point that you cannot see any technique?
a: Sometimes you can, like Sam Rockwell's physical life from Meisner training, but most great actors pull from many styles rather than one technique.

q: What is an acting exercise you learned early on that you still find most useful, and one that stands out as just ridiculous?
a: The useful one had two cameras catch whether you were truly listening and reacting to a partner, while the ridiculous ones included clucking around the room pretending to be a chicken.

q: Pound for pound, what is the greatest amusement park attraction for you?
a: A fast, looping roller coaster at Knott's Berry Farm that I rode with my mom three or four times in a row the day it opened.

q: How do you make your peanut butter and jelly sandwich, the build and the ratio?
a: Smooth peanut butter on both sides of the bread so the grape jam stays contained, a technique tied to fond childhood lunches with his mom.

q: What in your opinion makes for a good talk show interview?
a: The interviewer constantly adapts to make the other person feel comfortable, which is hard if you are already a pushover.

q: Where do you find your biggest critics?
a: Always yourself, because no one really knows what is going on in your personal life anyway.

q: What do you mean when you say embarrassment and humility are major sources of humor and creativity?
a: After her mom passed she didn't want to be a symbol of sorrow, and she found people laughed at her inadequacies, so self-awareness and self-deprecation became beautiful to her.

q: How intertwined is the food in New York City to what makes the city great?
a: So intertwined that New York wouldn't exist without its food culture; eating there prepares you for every other food city in the world.

q: For someone who has seen the light, what is most sacred about the trumpet?
a: It carries your own individual voice through it, so you can sing through it and blast people away with the volume.

q: At a dinner party where everyone shares their favorite YouTube videos, what are you typing into the search?
a: Maybe a how-to video on turning on a printer, or Vine videos, which she watches a lot of.

q: Are there any mushroom basics you can teach me right now?
a: Mushrooms can be grown into packaging shapes, clean up oil spills, and connect underground like the first internet, decomposing distant fallen trees.

q: What makes a Nintendo game like Super Mario 3D World one of the greatest achievements?
a: She loves how the games use optical illusions and the idea of other worlds and other ways of looking at the world.

q: What is a classic movie you would pull off the shelf for someone trying to heal a broken heart?
a: The Umbrellas of Cherbourg, because it is a pretty epic love story.

q: Which classic movie would you recommend to an adrenaline junkie who has only seen Marvel films?
a: Anything from the Bruce Lee catalog, like Enter the Dragon, for its passion, excitement, and artistry.

q: Did your experience doing interviews change how you approach being a talk show guest?
a: You have to bring real charm and genuine interest in the person, because audiences at home can see right through it when it isn't there.

q: You proactively chose to end your show when it could have run much longer. Can you talk through the why of that?
a: He understands how fast a show's legacy gets tarnished by overstaying, so he ended it while quality was high and people still loved it.

q: How do you describe the aesthetic appeal of Rick Owens to someone who knows nothing about fashion?
a: Everything looks dropped and elongated, with longer t-shirts and a much lower crotch, and it's the most comfortable clothing he's ever worn.

q: Are there any dos and don'ts when it comes to shopping for sweaters and their upkeep?
a: Know the quality of the fiber and how to store them, and keep cedar in the drawers to protect against moths.

q: What would shock me about what my talent agent says or does behind my back?
a: All those times they say they can't come to the phone or are out to lunch, they're not out to lunch and they can come to the phone; they just don't want to.

q: As a storyteller, how do you balance a film's high-minded ideas with the basic goal of entertaining people?
a: He aims for simple and special; like Michael Jackson singing ABC or Bob Marley singing No Woman No Cry, if you can say something simply, you understand it.

q: Which was a bigger thrill, winning a BAFTA or getting name-checked by Stormzy for winning it?
a: The Stormzy name-check, because you can't buy that and being shouted out by rappers had always been a dream.

q: You've said you needed to land one job out of every three. How did you come up with that math?
a: That early one-in-three rule kept him studying when he missed; his company name 59% comes from the win rate of the team in Moneyball.

q: What's the key to crying on camera?
a: He taps into sense memory, often a color he associates with a moment, then goes straight into that emotional space.

q: Are you thumbs up or thumbs down on Chicago-style deep dish pizza?
a: Thumbs down; he thinks pizza is overrated, basically just an open grilled-cheese sandwich.

q: How do you explain Britain's obsession with the quiz show format?
a: He loves that BBC quiz shows give away little money, so contestants really do it for the love of quizzing, and Jeopardy fills that void for him in America.

q: What is the magic of Alex Trebek as a quiz show host?
a: You could tell he loved the competition and genuinely enjoyed watching smart people do well, which is the essential joy of a quiz show.

q: Why have you decided to stay off social media?
a: He used to read comments about himself online, knows that is unhealthy, and doesn't think he is mentally strong enough for Twitter, which he is fine with.

q: Where did your trick of wearing the same jacket in public for months come from?
a: During a play the paparazzi waited at the same door nightly, so wearing identical clothes made every photo look like the same day and killed their value.

q: What is the best Dwayne Johnson film?
a: San Andreas, because the family storyline moved him more than it should have and Johnson makes every film better for being in it.

q: What's something you were super stressed about that turned out to be no big deal?
a: Whether anyone would care about a pop album dropping mid-pandemic; she realized she made Future Nostalgia to escape outside pressures, so the timing felt right.

q: How do you think about your own longevity as an artist?
a: She doesn't strategize it; her dream is to make music and perform for as long as people will have her.

q: What's a grocery or cooking hack for musicians on a tight budget?
a: Beans on toast.

q: What's an Albanian delicacy everyone should try before they die?
a: Ajvar, a slow-cooked roasted red pepper paste that goes on everything.

q: If you could collaborate with any artist from the disco era, living or dead, who would it be?
a: Nile Rodgers.

q: When you look at an album track list, what's your internal math for deciding which song to release as the first single?
a: He picks something that covers new ground fans wouldn't expect; on this album he swapped a sad lo-fi pick for an upbeat dance tune once England announced it was reopening.

q: Is there a song that turned out better because you followed your own conviction despite the feedback around you?
a: He actually got Shape of You wrong; he didn't want it on the album, thinking it felt like cheating, and it became the biggest hit of his career.

q: Is there an artist you're so jealous of that you check their stats all the time?
a: He says he isn't jealous of anyone because he loves what he does, but every artist tracks when Drake, Taylor, or Adele are releasing.

q: Where does doing a song with Eminem and 50 Cent rank among your career accomplishments?
a: Probably top three; he says no one at his school would ever have guessed the skinny, geeky kid would record with them.

q: Do you have an all-time favorite Ed in the Wild fan experience?
a: Bringing Courteney Cox to his tiny village's Tesco, which she thought was a farmer's market, so locals saw Monica from Friends browsing the pasta.

q: Why do you get joy out of auditioning?
a: You get to own a character before anyone else has an opinion on it, and the absurd, funny situation is a day spent doing the job you love.

q: What is your internal math on the cost-benefit of having Instagram?
a: The benefit is promoting projects that are hard to put out into the world, but she finds everything else about it detrimental.

q: What did being at Comic-Con teach you?
a: It moved her that people gathered there were so kind to one another over a shared love, and anything that brings people together that way is lovely.

q: What is one cookbook every home chef should have in their kitchen?
a: The El Buko cookbook, which collects recipes from the restaurant's chefs over the years.

q: What is the perfect classic movie for a night in with a bottle of wine?
a: The Conformist, for its great, beautiful visuals.

q: In layman's terms, what's the difference between a one-dollar gas station coffee and a five-dollar cup from a third-wave cafe?
a: Gas station coffee is one note, just a suggestion of coffee flavor, while a great cup is a longer, more exciting journey of flavors that you don't need to drown in milk and sugar.

q: What editing trope are you happy to have birthed to the masses, and one you never want to see again?
a: She's proud of recently slowing things down and cooling them off, and she can no longer stand the super-fast, jump-cut, zoom-heavy style she started with.

q: What's been the greatest triumph of your cooking tutorials, and your biggest fail?
a: Her triumph was discovering how easy it is to make your own nut milk, and her fail was a disgusting coffee cake from one of her earliest videos at age 16.

q: What distinguishes a good peanut butter from a mediocre one, and your pro tip for making it from scratch?
a: A good peanut butter is all natural, just peanuts and salt, because Jif tastes fake like icing, and when making it at home add a little vanilla bean.

q: As someone whose opinions change all the time, what's a recent bad take you've had on your own podcast?
a: She doesn't revisit anything she's said because it's true the moment she says it but might not be true tomorrow, and it's always ebbing and flowing.

q: What do you see as the value of humor or levity in a story that is very dark?
a: Humor is how people get through the hardest moments, and the lightness from friends and family in times of darkness is what makes survival possible.

q: Why is it one of your biggest pet peeves to watch a scene where an actor just moves food around the plate instead of eating it?
a: She loves actually eating and swallowing on camera, so she finds it frustrating when actors only push food around without taking a real bite.

q: Do you have a standout 'I never knew that is how it works' moment from your film career?
a: She is endlessly amazed by film sets, especially shooting on actual film, where a 'camera surgeon' arrives with a little surgical bag to take the camera apart and fix it.

q: What struck you the most when rehearsing alongside seasoned theater legends?
a: Seeing an acclaimed older actor knowing the whole play by heart yet still being as excited and nervous as she was, which taught her that if you get the opportunity you have to know your craft.

q: Do you have a favorite historic pub, and what makes it special?
a: Oxford's Turf Tavern, a legendary, tiny old building with low doorways and great ciders that feels like something out of Harry Potter.

q: Thinking back on your early touring days, what does a real character-builder of a nightmare show look like?
a: The first night of his Gazebo tour in Birmingham, Alabama, where the crowd was as small as the group he brought with him, but those humbling shows made him a better performer.

q: Is it true you made a song with your sixth-grade teacher?
a: Yes, his science teacher Mr. Swain was into rap and into the fact that Jack rapped, and as his student aide Jack would help grade papers while they chopped it up.

q: What's the secret to a good hip-hop ad-lib, and do you have a personal favorite of your own?
a: The best ones come instinctually by reacting to what you just said in the verse, and his fans' favorite is his "oof."

q: What do you look or listen for when you're picking out a beat?
a: He gravitates toward groovy, smooth stuff with a spin to it rather than an up-and-down bounce, shaped by what he grew up listening to.

q: Can you talk through the evolution of finding your voice as an artist?
a: He started recording before puberty with a very high voice, and like Andre 3000's advice to stop using a rap voice and just talk, speaking conversationally on the mic worked wonders for him.

q: Do you have a defense for the Australian burger after David Chang called them an abomination for putting beets on them?
a: He insists Australians make the best burgers because they pile on everything, eggs, bacon, avocado, the patty, six cheeses and mayo, though he draws the line at beets and pineapple.

q: Is there an actor who influenced your character development tactics, like keeping a journal from your character's perspective?
a: He cited James Dean's line about having Brando in one hand and Montgomery Clift in the other, wanting Clift's wounded sensitivity combined with Brando's brute force.

q: Off the top of your head, what's your favorite Brando movie and why?
a: He named On the Waterfront for the 'coulda been somebody' speech, though Sean noted that line is actually from The Godfather Part II.

q: How would you describe your relationship with playing a detestable character like Nate Jacobs?
a: He refuses to call his own character detestable and defends him, saying an actor has to open their heart and empathize, realizing even objectively shitty people come from circumstances different than yours.

q: What began your interest in photography, and why do you bring a camera on set?
a: A Heath Ledger documentary told through his own photos sparked it, and when he left home his mom bought him a Canon so he'd remember every beat of the journey.

q: What role do you think comedy instructors like Del Close have played in shaping American humor?
a: He calls it unquantifiable, describing a pyramid of influence like a coaching tree where mentors' styles get passed down and absorbed into your own.

q: You hated on Saturday Night Live as a young comedy lover before you were hired. How would you unpack that sentiment?
a: It was like hating on a crush; the disdain was really youthful arrogance masking his fear of never achieving something he badly wanted.

q: Has one of your reactions on this show ever become a meme or a gif?
a: He once nearly choked when a piece of chicken lodged in his windpipe across from Jon Bernthal, and briefly feared that would be his legacy.

q: What's your ideal Kansas City barbecue crawl?
a: Burnt ends from Gates, pulled pork from Kansas City Joe's, sides from Jack Stack, turkey from Q39, and brisket from Arthur Bryant's.

q: Do you have a favorite Norm Macdonald story?
a: Norm set him up with a long deadpan bit about his son saying Norm told jokes 'just like Seth,' purely to land a punchline that floored him.

q: What are the hallmarks of a good chase scene?
a: A good course with lots of rooms, handheld camerawork for chaos, a few stabs and injuries, and a shot where you see the heroine with the villain looming in the background.

q: Did you come out of your Tim Burton deep dive with a favorite production of his?
a: Beetlejuice is a staple she loves, but she championed the underrated Mars Attacks, saying she wanted to be one of the aliens as a kid.

q: What's the magic in collecting books for you?
a: As an insomniac she stays up writing essays, and reading writers like Emerson and following their tangents inspires her; she wishes people would read real books instead of iPads.

q: What's the secret to a perfect guacamole?
a: A whole lot of lime, plus jalapenos, cilantro, and good ripe avocados; she hates when people just toss a little pico de gallo on top.

q: What makes the '80s slasher Prom Night a gold standard of the genre?
a: It has all the classic teenage tropes and incredible gore, and it's inspired so many later horror films that it's the proper way to do a slasher.

q: Is intense scrutiny of a comedian's personal life a force for good or evil when writing new material?
a: The bigger peril is cannibalizing your own life; comedians start to eat their own tail, making jokes about old jokes and about being a comedian.

q: What's the worst reaction you ever got from an SNL host to a sketch you pitched them?
a: Josh Brolin flatly said it wasn't funny, which Mulaney found refreshing and valuable feedback.

q: What's the most satisfying part of comedy writing for you?
a: Writing a joke for someone else's award-show monologue and watching them land it; then telling whoever he's with, that was my joke.

q: Do you have a Mount Rushmore of comedy albums?
a: Mitch Hedberg's Strategic Grill Locations, Chris Rock's Bring the Pain, an Eddie Izzard album, and Dave Attell's Skanks for the Memories.

q: Did you ever have a goldfish as a kid?
a: No, but he riffed that feeding a fish is a tough test for people-pleasing children, since you want to feed it constantly but overfeeding can kill it.

q: As a reality TV creator, what 'how the sausage is made' details do you notice watching other shows that the rest of us miss?
a: She can tell when audio has been re-recorded or a phone call was faked, and notices small editing issues that fan accounts also catch.

q: What are the challenges of making a size-inclusive clothing line?
a: It costs far more in time and fabric to fit every body, and she won't let retailers carry her brand unless they take the full size range.

q: Do you have a go-to paparazzi evasion tactic, like prosthetics or disguises?
a: She and her sisters once wore prosthetics on a TMZ-style tour bus, but blew their own cover by slipping into fake accents.

q: What are your thoughts on how celebrity is handled in the media, and what's changed most over your time in the spotlight?
a: She enjoys interviews themselves but dreads the aftermath of being chopped up and reframed, which makes everything feel premeditated.

q: Is there an easy skincare hack you'd recommend for a hopeless guy whose cabinet is just shaving cream and bar soap?
a: Use Aquaphor or Vaseline around the eyes at night, stay consistent, and always wear sunscreen.

q: At the end of a long run playing a character, what's your relationship with leaving that character behind? Is it a relief or does it feel like a funeral?
a: He hasn't processed it yet and expects something like depression to hit, since he knows the character so well and still felt there was more he wanted to do.

q: From an actor's perspective, what's the most unforgivable thing an audience member can do during a live stage performance?
a: He recalled an old woman at a matinee loudly asking 'Is that the end?' during a long, scripted pause in his monologue.

q: Which professional wrestler do you think would have been the most successful on Broadway?
a: Batista, because his comedic range in Guardians of the Galaxy showed a screen presence his wrestling character never revealed.

q: What goes into putting together a Saturday Night Live monologue, especially a personal one?
a: He stressed over it more than anything else and focused entirely on it; the writers built it around a clip of his brother carrying him 30 years earlier.

q: Do you have a definitive Mount Rushmore of classic NES games?
a: Castlevania at number one, plus Castlevania II Simon's Quest and Mike Tyson's Punch-Out, which he insists you have to play on an old tube TV for the timing.

q: What's the longest you've ever taken to write a song from beginning to end?
a: Almost a year, like "Someone You Loved," which he started in October 2017 and didn't finish until September 2018, because for him songwriting means bashing his head against a wall to find one good melody.

q: What do you learn as an artist when a record's success exceeds your expectations?
a: That you don't know anything and nobody does, because the songs he thought were sure-fire smashes flopped, proving no one really knows what a hit is.

q: What's an appropriate amount of money to spend on a first date?
a: Nothing, because you might never meet that person again.

q: What's been your reaction to fans filming themselves sobbing over your music video?
a: He enjoys it, joking that he always wanted to go viral on TikTok and never thought he'd get there by traumatizing children, but if it pays the bills, so be it.

q: Do you have any foundational advice for people struggling with feelings of inadequacy even when life is otherwise going well?
a: He deflected with a deadpan joke, telling them they probably do suck and life is meaningless, rather than giving real advice.

q: For music fans, what are the pros and cons of an artist decoding their own lyrics?
a: As a lyric nut she loves reading them, but as she gets older she won't explain songs that feel too precious to commodify, and fans' deep annotations are sometimes more than she ever intended.

q: How do you think about creating distance between yourself and public attention?
a: It's both her introverted nature and a deliberate choice she made at 16 not to let validation change her day, because letting it in is a slippery slope.

q: As an aficionado of the art form, what are the keys to a perfect onion ring?
a: A thickly cut onion with a crisp yet thick battered crust, and crucially the onion must break cleanly on the bite instead of pulling out in threads.

q: Is there a theme or even a color you can identify in your most recent era of music?
a: Gold and golden-hour sun, because she thought of the album as a joyful sun-worship and devotional record inspired by her experiences in nature.

q: What do you find sacred about pop music?
a: The special challenge of speaking to a lot of people at once, and writing a melody that's simple yet secretly complex, which is a real experience you can't fake.

q: Can you explain to a non-scientist what we learn from Mars rovers like Curiosity and Perseverance?
a: They act as scouts for humanity, gathering data on water, radiation, wind, and temperature so that humans can eventually live on Mars.

q: What was one of the coolest moments of your life working at NASA?
a: Seeing the first grainy black-and-white image of the rover's shadow on the Martian surface after seven years of work, which made the whole control room erupt.

q: Is there a movie you tip your cap to for the way it depicts science or engineering?
a: The Martian, because Andy Weir did so much real research that even engineers who build rovers learned things from the book.

q: What makes a Frisbee fly?
a: Its angle of attack deflects air molecules downward, and the curved surface uses the Coanda effect so the Frisbee essentially jetpacks itself forward by throwing air down all around it.

q: What was the most eye-opening thing you learned about water pollution while helping clean up trash from rivers and oceans?
a: The people whose trash floods the rivers often have nowhere else to put it and live on landfills, so the real fix is solving the problem upstream rather than just scooping it up.

q: What is something people tend to over-think when shopping for a car, and something they do not think about enough?
a: People over-index on performance because a fast car is fun even though you do not need it, and they underrate the quality of the car's built-in software.

q: Is there a detail in a project that people thought was ridiculous while you were planning it, but that you are really proud of now?
a: He spent a lot to glue foam across an echoey studio ceiling, which looked absurd on the invoice but turned a seven-second clap echo into a room that simply sounds like people talking.

q: What is the most jaw-dropping thing you saw walking through a huge video production campus?
a: The sheer scale of the gear room, with shelves of cameras, batteries, SD cards, and microphones because a single challenge might equip a hundred people each with a full kit.

q: What YouTube rabbit hole or channel, besides your own, have you binged the most?
a: Over a lifetime it is Vsauce, whose videos he has watched basically all of, plus long multi-hour podcasts.

q: If you were in charge of YouTube for a day, what is a feature you would bring back or introduce?
a: He would bring back the public dislike counter and the old video-responses feature that let creators showcase replies beneath their videos.

q: What's the best anime to reach for when you need a laugh?
a: Black Clover; she loves the main character Asta, who was born with zero magic but beats everyone with anti-magic, and she relates to being counted out and proving people wrong.

q: How obsessive are you about sound mixing in the studio?
a: Very; it takes her at least 10 tries to mix a song perfectly and she records vocals about five times so her words land clearly with her Houston twang intact.

q: Do you have a hot girl tip for Mortal Kombat beginners?
a: Google a character's cheat codes, pick one move and memorize it, and you'll beat anyone; Mileena was the first character she ever looked up.

q: Do you have a lesson about breath control for aspiring performers?
a: Her mom taught her to take the breath at the right time instead of trying to spit it all out at once, catching it at the word that works for you.

q: What makes shooting a scene where your character is wolfing down food so much harder than an audience realizes?
a: You do it for 12 hours straight, so she keeps a bucket to spit into between takes and often just pretends to eat; she picks salad or fruit since she'll be eating it all day.

q: Are there British accents that you think should get more play in Hollywood?
a: The Liverpudlian accent; she loves it and supports Liverpool's football team.

q: What's an example of a Stranger Things storyline you're proudest to have put your fingerprints on?
a: Eleven's bullying arc this season, which she felt proud to represent as someone who has gone through bullying herself.

q: Of all the homework films, which one meant the most to you?
a: E.T., because it reminds her of Eleven: a being from another planet who knows no one and learns to trust, the way Eleven trusts Mike.

q: What's a whale quality you see most in yourself?
a: Humpback whales; she says they float and eat all day and like to be girls, which she relates to.

q: From a songwriting perspective, what's the key to an effective bridge?
a: It's often a tension-and-release departure that adds new information, and his hack is that if you can't land a good bridge, just go back and do the pre-chorus again.

q: As a coach on a singing competition show, how do you navigate the role that rejection plays?
a: That's the part he's struggled with, because he knows what it's like to be 16 on stage, so giving someone rejection feedback when the chair doesn't turn is the hard part.

q: How would you, as a coach today, judge your own audition from back in 2010?
a: He wouldn't have turned his chair for that guy, but he'd turn for himself now because he's learned a lot and been around the block.

q: Looking back on all the celebrity tournaments you've played, what's been your crowning golf achievement?
a: Making five real-life birdies on the back nine at Riviera in the Genesis Open with his friend Justin Rose, which helped them win the pro-am.

q: If someone handed you an acoustic guitar in a country pub, what's your go-to song to win the room over?
a: "The Fields of Athenry," a big Irish pub anthem that always gets sung along to anywhere in Ireland.

q: Do you remember the first local talent show you ever won?
a: A Boys and Girls Club singing contest where she won a trophy as tall as her and a $100 check, which felt like a fortune at eight years old.

q: How do you think about blending novelty and nostalgia in your art?
a: She's a very visual artist who pictures each song, and she's obsessed with the '90s and its music and pop culture, though being born in 2003 means a Gen Z streak shows up too.

q: How do you know whether a feeling or idea fits best in a pop punk song versus a heartbreak ballad versus an alt rock anthem?
a: She doesn't keep it linear since some of her favorite music is upbeat with depressing lyrics; she's lyric centered and writes every idea down on her phone first.

q: What's your insight on putting together a great bridge?
a: A bridge should add a new perspective the rest of the song didn't cover; she likes hers either quiet and introspective or really big, since she likes songs to be all over the place.

q: What's your response to people who think an oatmeal raisin cookie can never be better than chocolate chip?
a: She hates chocolate chip cookies and defends oatmeal raisin as the best, arguing they're hearty and somewhat healthy because oatmeal has fiber.

q: What's the weirdest product or licensing pitch you've ever had to put your name on?
a: A pink can of 'Paris Hilton's beans,' and separately an adult-themed product line she refused.

q: As a pioneer of influencer culture, what makes you proud of it and what makes you think it's gotten out of control?
a: She's proud people can build a living just being themselves, but worries about young girls chasing likes and basing self-worth on strangers' opinions.

q: What's the story about outwitting the paparazzi that you're most proud of?
a: She dressed a friend in a blonde wig and her tracksuit with a chihuahua to bolt from her house as a decoy, a trick she's pulled about 50 times.

q: The street artist Banksy took 500 copies of your debut album and doctored them with remixes. Positive or negative reaction?
a: She thought it was sick and was flattered, and has since been hunting auctions to buy one of the doctored copies.

q: As an actor, how do you balance the noble traits that make a character heroic with the weaker ones that make him human?
a: He pointed to the male archetype of a restricted exterior that hides feelings to survive, only to discover the character cannot live without human connection.

q: When you watch other movies or shows, do you think about how heavy or uncomfortable an actor's wardrobe might be?
a: Yes, he is so hyper-aware of what the cast and crew endure, like blisters from bad shoes, that his family wants to kill him during movies.

q: Do you have a Mount Rushmore of Nicolas Cage movies?
a: Raising Arizona, Peggy Sue Got Married, Face/Off, and Vampire's Kiss, the early ones that shaped him as an aspiring actor.

q: What makes Cap'n Crunch the top dog of breakfast cereals for you?
a: Its own caramel-like taste makes him think about his childhood, which he would do anything to go back to.

q: Why is Prince's Purple Rain the song you'd most want to hear at your funeral?
a: It is his favorite and most emotionally cathartic song; he says he was raised by HBO, Spielberg, and Prince.

q: You've talked about losing your passion for music. What changed?
a: Music had become a job buried under labels and touring, but stripping back to just a couple of producers and artists made it feel like emerging from a cocoon as a butterfly.

q: Have you had any close encounters with wildlife since moving to your secluded mountain compound?
a: A crafty mama cougar keeps getting through his big fence, and he swears his infrared cameras also caught a glowing orb he thinks was an alien or a ghost.

q: What are the conversations like when you're tricking out a custom guitar or choosing instruments for a tour?
a: He favors the road-worn look and backstory, like a 1938 Martin that came with a rattlesnake tail inside the case, and Willie Nelson's guitar with a hole punched in it.

q: What's the biggest misconception about Magic: The Gathering and the people who play it?
a: That it's nerdy, but he's happily a nerd who'd rather sit in his room playing cards all day than go to a strip club, and it's pretty normal.

q: Can you give me a ghost-hunting starter pack?
a: An EMF detector, a voice recorder (preferably analog), and a camera with infrared or night-shot, and then you're good to go.

q: How would you describe British pub culture to someone who has never experienced it?
a: They are beautiful, low-key, soulful places with quiz nights, great for a date, and there is always an old regular in the corner.

q: Who do you think is the musician who had the biggest influence on changing the way that people dress?
a: Kanye West came to mind first, and then Rihanna, whose album Loud made wearing affordable high street brands feel exciting.

q: Do you ever think of your saddest songs as actually being your happiest songs?
a: Yes, because turning something painful into something wonderful brings a lot of joy and reminds people they are not alone.

q: Do you remember the first face-to-face meeting you ever had with your hero Elton John?
a: He invited me to his London home as a new artist, we had tea and got on so well, and he has been an honest, guiding figure ever since.

q: What is perfection to you, and how do we stop expecting it from ourselves?
a: There is no perfect destination free of pain, so the goal is accepting that happiness is fluid and fleeting and embracing all of your emotions.

q: What takes a hit single and propels it into a viral dance craze?
a: She credits relatability; she talks about normal things people connect with, so it's nice to see they like what she likes.

q: What advice would you give a young artist entering their first label deal? Are there sneaky terms to look out for?
a: Ask for an expanded contract rather than the condensed deal labels offer, because a lawyer who isn't aware won't catch it.

q: Where do you stand on the great oyster debate of raw versus cooked?
a: She loves raw oysters.

q: What's the most oysters you've ever eaten in one sitting?
a: Two dozen, one for an appetizer and one to close out dinner.

q: Do you think there's value in listening to your critics?
a: Yes, when they actually have something constructive to say, not when it's just gruesome hate.

q: What excites you most about acting in a scene with very little dialogue?
a: He's drawn to the person who does less; saying less lets the audience project their own ideas onto the character and stay fascinated.

q: Why should every actor take a screenwriting class?
a: Breaking down script analysis makes you a better actor and helps you see which scenes really matter and what you're trying to say.

q: How important is formal acting training for an aspiring actor?
a: Very important; he says you have to study, train, and work on your craft, especially in theater.

q: If you did a destination travel-and-food show, where would you shoot the first episode?
a: Japan.

q: What recent discovery or space fact fascinates you the most?
a: The idea of colonizing Mars; he read that satellites the size of football stadiums could melt the ice into water so people could live there.

q: What do you find sacred about short films?
a: They are affordable, so a starving artist can make one as a proof of concept and a calling card for bigger opportunities.

q: Is there anything you learned at your old job that still helps you today?
a: He learned he was a terrible accountant, with such a high error rate that his spreadsheets came back marked all in red, which confirmed it was not his calling.

q: What is the strangest place you have ever seen a stock photo of yourself pop up in real life?
a: A photo of him doing Zumba was on the wall of a martial arts dojo, and he was also on the cover of an accounting textbook.

q: Do you have an all-time favorite Star Wars Jedi?
a: Qui-Gon Jinn, because he is a maverick who breaks the rules and sees through the hypocrisy of the Jedi Order.

q: What is the next thing you want to speak into existence?
a: Reviving LX4, the boy band he started with three friends in middle school, since he is already working on music.

q: When choosing where to go, how much should you weigh prestige versus finding the place that's the best fit for you?
a: He says go where the best fit is rather than chasing pedigree, crediting his own success to getting real reps instead of riding the bench at a bigger program.

q: A famous 1985 study argued the 'hot hand' or being on fire is a myth. Does being on fire feel real to you?
a: Absolutely; he describes moments where everything else disappears and the rim feels like the ocean, calling the study wrong.

q: In your opinion, what team staff role is most important to keeping things moving but doesn't get enough credit?
a: The injury management and prevention staff, who balance short-term comebacks against long-term longevity so athletes can perform for many years.

q: What makes a textbook-pure shooting form, and which flaws in someone's mechanics are fixable versus fatal?
a: A stacked form with power built from the feet up is repeatable; the deadly sin is the 'chicken wing' elbow, which wrecks your arc and power.

q: How much did it mean that Kobe Bryant picked up on your 'baby-faced assassin' nickname?
a: It is one of his favorite memories; Kobe saw the killer instinct hidden behind his smile and validated it without needing to say a word.

q: Do you have any swing tips for hopeless duffers trying to get good at golf?
a: Shorten your backswing and don't try to hit it too hard, since modern clubs are advanced enough that the ball travels far without much force.

q: What are the limitations or opportunities for an actor when half a movie is spent hidden behind a mask?
a: You lose facial expression and must convey emotion through physicality, so bigger reactions that would look crazy unmasked read as normal; his trick is letting his arms swing loosely to keep Spider-Man youthful.

q: Can you explain what a carvery is for the uninitiated?
a: A pub meal built around a carved roast, like a roast dinner.

q: What does it mean if a dish is minging?
a: It means it's disgusting, like that last wing was minging.

q: What can an actor learn going straight into the industry that they might miss at a prestigious drama school?
a: Going in fresh lets directors become your teachers; he learns his lines the morning of so they're as fresh as possible when he says them, advice he credits to Robert Downey Jr.
